mechanism: Selective FLT3 tyrosine kinase inhibition (targets FLT3‑ITD)
additional_notes: Alternative name: ASP2215; FDA‑approved for FLT3‑mutant AML; suggested 10‑point 3‑fold dilution from 10 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Potent FLT3 tyrosine kinase inhibition (highly active against FLT3‑ITD)
additional_notes: Alternative name: AC220; very potent TKI — suggested top conc 1 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Multi‑kinase inhibition including FLT3; broad kinase inhibitory profile
additional_notes: Alternative name: PKC412; approved for FLT3‑mutant AML; suggested 10 µM to 1 nM range.

mechanism: Multi‑kinase inhibition including FLT3/RAF/VEGFR pathways
additional_notes: Used off‑label in FLT3 AML; suggested 10 µM to 0.1 nM range.

mechanism: Broad tyrosine kinase inhibition including FLT3 and BCR‑ABL family
additional_notes: Has reported activity in FLT3‑ITD models; suggested 1 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Selective BCL‑2 inhibition promoting apoptosis in AML cells
additional_notes: Alternative name: ABT‑199; clinically active in AML, often tested in combinations.

mechanism: Pyrimidine nucleoside analogue that inhibits DNA synthesis (S‑phase specific)
additional_notes: Also called Ara‑C; standard‑of‑care positive control; suggested 100 µM to 1 nM.

mechanism: DNA methyltransferase inhibitor leading to hypomethylation and gene reactivation
additional_notes: May require longer exposure (5–7 days) for epigenetic effects; suggested 100 µM to 1 nM.

mechanism: Incorporates into RNA/DNA and inhibits DNA methyltransferases, altering gene expression
additional_notes: Used in AML/MDS; longer exposures advisable; suggested 100 µM to 1 nM.

mechanism: Reversible inhibition of the 26S proteasome leading to proteotoxic stress and apoptosis
additional_notes: Reported activity and synergy in AML cell lines; suggested 1 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Cyclin‑dependent kinase inhibition (e.g., CDK9) leading to transcriptional suppression and apoptosis
additional_notes: Also called flavopiridol; investigated in AML clinical research.

mechanism: Inhibits protein translation (ribosomal function) causing cytotoxicity in hematologic malignancies
additional_notes: Also called homoharringtonine; used in hematologic cancers; suggested 10 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Allosteric MEK1/2 inhibition, blocking MAPK signaling downstream of RAS
additional_notes: Relevant for RAS‑activated AML subsets; suggested 1 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: mTOR complex 1 inhibition reducing protein synthesis and cell growth
additional_notes: Temsirolimus is an alternative; mTOR inhibitors often used in combinations; suggested 10 µM to 1 nM.

mechanism: Inhibits SRC/ABL and other kinases, affecting proliferation and survival
additional_notes: Has some activity in AML cell lines; useful in combination studies; suggested 1 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Reported mitochondrial uncoupling and inhibition of Wnt/STAT3 pathways among others, causing cytotoxicity
additional_notes: Low solubility — ensure formulation; repeatedly identified in repurposing screens; suggested 10 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Microtubule disruption and other proposed anti‑cancer effects leading to mitotic arrest and death
additional_notes: Hit in repurposing screens for AML; suggested 10 µM to 0.1 nM.

mechanism: Inhibits HMG‑CoA reductase affecting cholesterol biosynthesis and can induce apoptosis/anti‑proliferative effects
additional_notes: Listed as representative statin; lower potency reported in many studies — suggested top 50 µM (50,000 nM) to 10 nM.

mechanism: Impacts Hedgehog/VEGFR/mTOR signaling and other off‑target pathways leading to anticancer activity
additional_notes: Found in repurposing screens with anticancer signals; suggested 10 µM to 0.1 nM.

category: ALDH inhibitor / metal‑dependent cytotoxic agent (repurposing candidate)
mechanism: ALDH inhibition and copper‑dependent proteotoxicity/oxidative stress causing cancer cell death
additional_notes: Test ± Cu2+ (e.g., low µM CuCl2) as activity is often copper‑dependent; suggested 10 µM to 0.1 nM.
